case_name: Van Gend en Loos v Nederlandse Administratie der Belastingen
court: Court of Justice of the European Communities
citation: Case 26/62, Van Gend en Loos [1963] ECR 1
text: VAN GEND EN LOOS (1963). COURT: Court of Justice of the European Communities. FACTS: Van Gend en Loos, a Dutch transport company, challenged an increase in customs duties imposed by the Netherlands on imports from Germany, arguing that it violated Article 12 of the EEC Treaty (now Article 30 TFEU), which prohibited member states from introducing new customs duties or increasing existing ones. The Dutch court referred the question to the ECJ. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether provisions of the EEC Treaty could create rights for individuals that national courts must protect. (2) Whether Article 12 EEC had DIRECT EFFECT. HOLDING: The Court held that Article 12 EEC was directly effective — it created individual rights that national courts were bound to protect, even in the absence of implementing legislation. REASONING: The Court stated that the Community constitutes a 'new legal order of international law' for the benefit of which states have limited their sovereign rights. The subjects of this legal order comprise not only member states but also their nationals. Community law therefore creates rights for individuals which become part of their legal heritage. The test for direct effect: a provision must be clear, precise, unconditional, and require no further legislative implementation. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Established the doctrine of DIRECT EFFECT — the most foundational principle of EU law. (2) Created the concept of EU law as a 'new legal order' distinct from traditional international law. (3) Transformed the European Communities from an international organization into a supranational legal order with rights enforceable by individuals.

court: Court of Justice of the European Communities
text: COSTA v ENEL (1964). COURT: Court of Justice of the European Communities. FACTS: Flaminio Costa, an Italian lawyer and shareholder in Edison Volta, challenged Italy's nationalization of the electricity sector (creating ENEL), arguing it violated several provisions of the EEC Treaty. The Italian court referred questions to the ECJ. Italy argued that as the nationalization law was enacted after the EEC Treaty, it must prevail under the lex posterior principle. HOLDING: The Court held that EU law takes precedence over conflicting national law, including subsequent national legislation. REASONING: By creating a Community of unlimited duration, with its own institutions, personality, and legal capacity, the member states limited their sovereign rights and created a body of law that binds both their nationals and themselves. The integration of Community law into the legal systems of the member states makes it impossible for states to accord precedence to a unilateral and subsequent national measure over a legal system accepted by them on a basis of reciprocity. EU law must prevail because allowing national law to override Community law would undermine the uniform application of EU law. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Established the SUPREMACY (PRIMACY) OF EU LAW — EU law prevails over all conflicting national law, including subsequent legislation. (2) Together with Van Gend en Loos, forms the constitutional foundation of the EU legal order. (3) One of the most important judicial decisions in European integration.

case_name: Internationale Handelsgesellschaft v Einfuhr- und Vorratsstelle für Getreide und Futtermittel
court: Court of Justice of the European Communities
text: INTERNATIONALE HANDELSGESELLSCHAFT (1970). COURT: Court of Justice. The Court held that EU law takes precedence even over fundamental rights provisions of national constitutions. The validity of Community measures can only be judged in light of Community law, not national constitutional law. However, fundamental rights form an integral part of EU law as general principles. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Extended the SUPREMACY doctrine over national CONSTITUTIONAL PROVISIONS. (2) Established that FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS are general principles of EU law. (3) Catalyzed the development of EU human rights protection.

case_name: Factortame Ltd v Secretary of State for Transport
court: Court of Justice (and House of Lords)
citation: Case C-213/89, R v Secretary of State for Transport, ex parte Factortame [1990] ECR I-2433
text: FACTORTAME (1990). COURT: Court of Justice of the European Communities / House of Lords. FACTS: Spanish fishermen challenged the UK Merchant Shipping Act 1988, which imposed nationality and residence requirements for fishing vessel registration, as contrary to EU law. The House of Lords asked the ECJ whether national courts must set aside a national statute that may be incompatible with EU law. HOLDING: The ECJ held that national courts must set aside any national legislative provision that prevents the granting of interim relief in a case concerning EU rights. National courts must be able to grant interim relief against the Crown even where an Act of Parliament is involved. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Confirmed that national courts must set aside conflicting national legislation to give effect to EU law. (2) Revolutionary for UK constitutional law — effectively required UK courts to disapply an Act of Parliament. (3) Demonstrated the practical supremacy of EU law over parliamentary sovereignty.

court: Court of Justice of the European Communities
citation: Joined Cases C-6/90 and C-9/90, Francovich [1991] ECR I-5357
text: FRANCOVICH v ITALY (1991). COURT: Court of Justice. FACTS: Italy failed to implement Directive 80/987/EEC protecting employees in the event of employer insolvency. Andrea Francovich and others were left without protection when their employers became insolvent. Since the directive lacked direct effect (it was not sufficiently precise and unconditional), they could not rely on it directly. HOLDING: The Court established the principle of STATE LIABILITY — member states are liable in damages to individuals for their failure to implement EU directives, provided: (1) the directive confers rights on individuals; (2) the content of those rights can be identified from the directive; and (3) there is a causal link between the breach and the damage suffered. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Created the doctrine of STATE LIABILITY for breach of EU law. (2) Provided a remedy where direct effect was unavailable. (3) Strengthened the enforcement of EU law through private litigation.

case_name: Cassis de Dijon (Rewe-Zentral AG v Bundesmonopolverwaltung für Branntwein)
court: Court of Justice of the European Communities
citation: Case 120/78, Rewe-Zentral AG v Bundesmonopolverwaltung für Branntwein [1979] ECR 649
text: CASSIS DE DIJON (1979). COURT: Court of Justice. FACTS: A German company sought to import the French blackcurrant liqueur Cassis de Dijon into Germany. German law required fruit liqueurs to have a minimum alcohol content of 25%, while Cassis de Dijon had only 15-20%. Germany refused the import. HOLDING: The Court held that any product lawfully produced and marketed in one member state must be admitted to the market of any other member state (MUTUAL RECOGNITION), unless the importing state can justify restrictions on grounds of mandatory requirements (public health, consumer protection, fiscal supervision, fairness of commercial transactions). SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Established the principle of MUTUAL RECOGNITION — goods lawfully marketed in one member state must be accepted in others. (2) Created the MANDATORY REQUIREMENTS doctrine as a justification for restrictions. (3) Foundation of the EU single market for goods.
